findings.reachability: Authenticated administrators and shop managers only (wp_ajax_ with current_user_can check). Not reachable by unauthenticated users.
findings.justification: $_REQUEST['phrase'] is assigned to $phrase with no sanitization (no sanitize_text_field, no wp_kses, no trim/strip), then passed directly into getSearchResults($phrase, true, 'autocomplete'); while the function's downstream handling determines actual impact, the raw unsanitized value crosses a trust boundary unnecessarily for an admin-only handler.

detection_pattern.section_name: Missing sanitization: raw $_REQUEST phrase passed to business logic
detection_pattern.description: Catches handlers that extract a 'phrase' parameter from $_REQUEST without any sanitization function call on the same line. Files matching the grep pattern but not the anti-pattern may be passing raw user input into search, database, or rendering functions.

findings.reachability: Authenticated administrators and shop managers only (wp_ajax_ with current_user_can check). Not reachable by unauthenticated users.
findings.justification: $phrase = (!empty($_REQUEST['phrase']) ? $_REQUEST['phrase'] : '') is passed unsanitized to $data->markAsSolved($phrase). The markAsSolved method uses $wpdb->update() with format specifiers (['%s']), so SQL injection is prevented by parameterization; however, the raw string with no sanitize_text_field call violates WordPress coding standards and could carry unexpected content (null bytes, control characters) into the database.

detection_pattern.section_name: Unsanitized phrase from request passed to DB write
detection_pattern.description: Catches AJAX handlers writing a raw $_REQUEST['phrase'] value to the database without sanitize_text_field. Even when $wpdb->update/insert uses format specifiers (preventing SQL injection), the absence of sanitization allows null bytes or oversized strings to be stored.
